claim: A skill is a portable unit of agent behavior that teaches any AI tool how to do a specific job, written once and usable across Claude, Codex, Cursor, and other agents without changing a line
actionable: Define skills as reusable markdown files with YAML frontmatter (name, description) plus optional scripts to make your workflows portable across tools
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser

claim: The skill description is not for humans but for the LLM to determine at runtime when to use the skill automatically—it is the routing mechanism
actionable: Write descriptions that explain when a skill should be applied (e.g., 'roast this repository and get a fun analysis') rather than how to do the task
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser

claim: Providing a few constraints is more powerful than being overly prescriptive about how to do a task—specify what not to do rather than exactly how to do it
actionable: Use constraints like 'never be vague' or 'when you cite code, always include line numbers and git commit reference' instead of lengthy prescriptive instructions
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser

claim: Script interpolation with backticks allows deterministic injection of command output (like '!`git log`') which Claude will execute and substitute, unlike asking the LLM to guess at data
actionable: Use !`command` syntax in skills to inject exact command output instead of relying on the LLM to fetch or guess at data—this is token-efficient and deterministic
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser

claim: Progressive disclosure allows you to conditionally load additional markdown files (e.g., testing.md only during testing tasks) so context window isn't bloated with irrelevant information
actionable: Reference local paths to additional markdown files in skills that are only loaded when relevant, preventing context bloat from always-available but rarely-needed information
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser

claim: Asking Claude to use the built-in skill-builder or skill-creator skill is an efficient way to review, critique, and improve your skills and run evaluation tests
actionable: When developing a skill, use the command '/skill-builder' or ask Claude to analyze and critique your skill definition to identify improvements and run evals
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser

claim: Skills stored in .claude/skills folder in a repo are automatically loaded; global skills in ~/.claude/skills apply everywhere; marketplaces standardize skill distribution via NPX
actionable: Place repo-specific skills in .claude/skills/<skill-name>/skill.md for local loading, or in ~/.claude/skills for global availability, or publish to a marketplace for team sharing
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser

claim: Overly prescriptive skills can harm performance—adding specific constraints led to a 30% drop in accuracy for the Next.js installer skill because Claude was already good at it
actionable: Measure skill performance with evals before and after adding constraints; remove prescriptive rules if they reduce accuracy—less is sometimes more
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser

claim: Confidence scoring forces iterative loops where the LLM assesses confidence (e.g., 0-100 based on problem clarity, goal definition, success criteria) and asks clarifying questions until confidence threshold is met
actionable: Add confidence rubrics to skills that score certainty across dimensions (clarity, goal definition, scope) and trigger follow-up questions when below threshold (e.g., 90/100)
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser

claim: Sub-agents are useful when you want a standalone context window separate from the main task, allowing the sub-agent to do work (like code reviews) without eating the parent task's context
actionable: Use sub-agents for isolated tasks that require feedback loops or extensive processing; they report back without bloating the main context window
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser

claim: Distinguishing between claude.md/agents.md (loaded globally every time) and skills (loaded conditionally): put only tiny global rules in .md files and move specific workflows into skills to avoid context bloat
actionable: Keep claude.md minimal (e.g., 'use pnpm' as a one-liner); put project-specific rules, testing procedures, and complex logic into separate skills loaded only when needed
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser

claim: Shared team skills create governance challenges: without versioning or curation, teams end up with duplicate skills with slightly different descriptions, skill bloat, and confusion about which skill to use
actionable: For shared skills, use plugin interfaces with versioning (like NPM packages), publish to a single authoritative repository, and document when each skill should be used to prevent duplication
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser

claim: The CLI pattern using Cloud Agent SDK can power 15+ integrations by composing skills—each integration is a skill, and the CLI routes requests to the appropriate skill-based workflow
actionable: Design CLIs with an agent SDK backend where each command is a skill; this allows adding integrations without changing CLI code, just adding new skills to the skill directory
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser

claim: Image generation and video creation skills can be chained: first skill generates a static image, second skill animates it via API (e.g., VEO), enabling complex workflows from a single user prompt
actionable: Chain simple skills together—use one skill to generate a base artifact (image, text, data) and another to transform or augment it (animate, format, extend)
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser

claim: Skills are easily shareable as .skill files (zipped skill folders renamed) that non-technical users can drag into Claude Desktop without needing version control or code knowledge
actionable: Zip a skill folder and rename .zip to .skill to create a portable unit that any user can drag into Claude Desktop; no git, CLI, or technical setup required
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser

claim: Analyzing your own past conversation logs (JSONL files) is gold for skill iteration—look for what frustrated you, what failed, and use that to identify edge cases and new skill opportunities
actionable: Periodically review saved conversation transcripts to find patterns of friction; ask Claude to suggest skills that could have solved those problems smoothly
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser

claim: Evals (evaluation frameworks) should measure if a skill actually improves performance over baseline—if skill performance is only 1-2% better than without, it may not be worth the complexity
actionable: Run evals comparing results with skill vs. without skill; require skills to improve accuracy by 80%+ or show meaningful improvement, then re-eval when new models drop
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser

claim: Skills with many loaded skills may get ignored by the LLM if descriptions aren't specific enough—include acronyms, trigger keywords, and use / slash commands when you want a specific skill guaranteed
actionable: Make skill descriptions include all expected acronyms and keywords for reliable routing; document how to invoke a skill by name with /skill-name if auto-routing fails
source_title: Full Walkthrough: Writing & Using Skills — Nick Nisi and Zack Proser
